detailsTitle: "Dino Miss Torino '66, la più sognata - ecco la versione Ferrari-Pininfarina", in "Autosprint", year VI, no. 45, October 31 - November 7, 1966
detailsDescription: Dino Miss Torino '66, la più sognata - ecco la versione Ferrari-Pininfarina, in "Autosprint" (tr. Dino Miss Turin '66, the most desired - introducing the Ferrari-Pininfarina version), year VI, no. 45, October 31 - November 7, 1966, pp. 12-13.     The article presents the Dino Ferrari-Pininfarina 206 GT as one of the main novelties at the Turin Motor Show (Salone di Torino). It is described as a "young" and "highly ambitious car," which aims to compete in the Gran Turismo (G.T.) segment against the Porsche 911.  The car is an evolution of the Fiat Dino concept, but it is distinguished by the Pininfarina design and the transparent trellis structure, which makes it a more expensive and complex competitor compared to other models.     The front end design was studied for maximum aerodynamic resistance, with the headlight groups set further back than previous versions to lengthen the profile of the fenders. The hood is light, made of fiberglass (VTR), with a rear slot for the hot air outlet from the radiator.  The windscreen is wide and inclined, while the roofline is very thin and reduced in size. The rear section houses the engine, and the trunk section contains the luggage compartment and a cowl for the exit of heated air from the engine assembly.  The dashboard and padded interior components are made with anti-shock and anti-glare materials.     The article is also accompanied by some technical data of the Dino 206 GT.

detailsTitle: "Si chiama Dino, si legge Desiderio. Fiat o Ferrari", in "Autosprint", year VI, no. 46, November 7-14, 1966
detailsDescription: Si chiama Dino, si legge Desiderio. Fiat o Ferrari (tr. It's called Dino, it spells Desire. Fiat or Ferrari), in "Autosprint", year VI, no. 46, November 7-14, 1966, pp. 14-15.     The article introduces the FIAT DINO Spider as the car resulting from the March 1965 agreement between Fiat and Ferrari. Fiat committed to reproducing the Dino engine to allow its use in the Ferrari Formula 2 single-seater.  Fiat emphasized that this collaboration is a concrete demonstration of its return to Italian sports car racing, even without direct involvement in the races, a fact that signifies its significant connection to Fiat's past racing traditions.  The Dino Spider, born from the racing world, has been re-elaborated for series production and is mounted on a car designed for comfort, safety, and suitability for the good motorist. The bodywork for the Spider was done by Pininfarina.  The FIAT DINO Spider was presented in Turin and will be followed in the spring by a coupé version by Bertone. The dimensions of the Spider are large enough that they might affect the production of the 2500 S coupé. The first deliveries of the Spider are not expected before January 1967 due to the necessary fine-tuning of its components.     The presentation of the Ferrari DINO 206 GT Coupé designed by Pininfarina was perhaps more successful than that of the standard Fiat version. Ferrari, in fact, has brought the DINO prototype to Maranello for a chain-production setup that anticipates building 120 units per month starting in December 1967.  The prototype was completed in record time. Even Ferrari did not hide his satisfaction, partly because the car's launch avoided a "nuisance" last-minute introduction at the Salon in early November.  The prototype, painted yellow with blue interiors, has demonstrated a clearer aesthetic distinction from the FIAT model, retaining the key difference of the rear-mounted engine. The car is positioned as an anti-Porsche vehicle and its price is expected to be under 3 million lire. In contrast, the fixed price for the FIAT DINO, which may also compete with Alfa Romeo, seems set to exceed 3 million lire.

Terremoto Maranello Autosprint year VI no. 52 December 19-26 1966 la fine del mondo! open letter by Enzo Ferrari in Autosprint year VI no. 52 December 19-26 1966 on the cover. Text transcription (italian) Per un momento abbiamo temuto di avere commesso un marchiano errore infatti articolo pubblicato venerdì scorso sul «Corriere della Sera» denunciava clamorosamente che «il mondo dell automobile» si posto un interrogativo Ferrari ha avuto paura? Occorre spiegare Ferrari avrebbe avuto quest anno paura di sottoporsi alle domande della stampa nella conferenza annuale che veniva organizzata in dicembre Modena. Sarebbe così ricorso all accorgimento di parlare alla televisione anzi per dirla come stata scritta si sarebbe attaccato alle gonne mamma televisione. Il «mondo dell automobile» stupito attonito questa prassi inusitata si sarebbe indignato chiederebbe riparazione. Noi ci siamo preoccupati perché in effetti anche noi facciamo poco parte del mondo dell automobile ci sarebbe veramente dispiaciuto se per ventura avessimo mostrata non saperci stare questo mondo. Abbiamo precisato il programma 1967 della attraverso una trasmissione televisiva. In trentadue minuti grazie all organizzazione offerta dal Dott. Assetta direzione RAI-TV Torino ci stato consentito esporre le nostre previsioni per la incipiente stagione sportiva. Dipendesse solo da noi non avremmo fatto neanche questa perché nella nostra età quella che veramente interessa se ne siamo ancora capaci onorare pista il nostro Sport. Tutto il resto lo abbiamo ripetutamente affermato appartiene ad ruolo viene troppo spesso perentoriamente attribuito noi invece respingiamo. stato sentenziato ormai «res publica» non può sottrarsi certi doveri ed impegni ha obbligo informare dare notizia su tutto quello sta facendo sulle automobili costruisce sui piloti ingaggiati sugli impianti realizzati sul personale assunto sul tempo fa su quello farà! Tutto quello riguarda Maranello fa notizia bisogna farne sapere altrimenti «mondo automobile» indigna protesta minaccia attua rappresaglie. Il bello «mondo vorrebbe primizie «qualche cosa abbiano tutti». Non siamo mai chiesti ascoltando queste profferire se tratti amore giornale attivismo professionale caccia alla bracconiera volontà ambiziosa nel risultare primo classe. Non lo sappiamo preferiamo saperlo. Quest anno proprio evitare supponibili parzialità abbiamo accettato invito TV risposto alle domande dei rappresentanti stampa da essa invitati. Se nostra fiacchezza ha divertito poco male mestiere. Eventualmente anno prossimo pregheremo Gino Bramieri Isabella Biagini elettrizzare botta risposta sui programmi 1968. Ma forse punto questo. altro dobbiamo rispondere all interrogativo poi mondo ma soltanto stampa quel anzi parte quella meglio quotidiano anche «Corriere Sera» alla fine cosa resta? Un collega ama prefabbricare piloti casa viaggiare su automobili straniere. Non fa niente. Risponderemo esaurientemente nove «drammatiche» domande Lorenzo Pilogallo perché vogliamo deluderlo batterci nel 67 con Ford scritto pazzesco ma trovassimo contro anche ingegnere Pilogallo proprio fine. English translation For moment we feared we had committed serious error fact the article published last Friday the «Corriere Sera» dramatically denounced that «the automotive world» had posed question Was afraid? It must be explained was allegedly afraid this year of submitting to press questions at annual conference that was organized every December Modena. It supposedly resorted to ploy of speaking on television—or to quote exactly how it was written it clung apron strings of mommy television. The «automotive world» is astonished and amazed by this unusual practice it is allegedly indignant and would demand reparations. We became concerned because effect we too are somewhat part automotive world and would be truly displeased if happened show that don know how behave this world. We clarified 1967 program through televised broadcast. In thirty-two minutes thanks organization offered by Dr. Assetta RAI-TV management Turin were able present our forecasts for upcoming racing season. If were solely up us wouldn have even done because at our age what truly interests us is if are still capable honor our Sport on track. All rest as have repeatedly stated belongs role too often peremptorily attributed us which instead reject. It has been decreed now «res publica» cannot shirk certain duties commitments has obligation inform give notice everything doing about cars builds drivers hires facilities creates personnel hires weather what will do! Everything concerning Maranello makes news people must be informed otherwise «automotive world» becomes indignant protests threatens enacts reprisals. The funny thing «automotive wants scoops «something not everyone has.» We have never asked listening these pronouncements whether about love for newspaper professional activism chasing after poachers or an ambitious desire come out first class. don know prefer not know. This precisely avoid conceivable partiality accepted invitation TV answered questions from representatives press invited them. If lack flair not entertaining no matter are professionals. Perhaps next will ask Gino Bramieri Isabella Biagini electrify back-and-forth about 1968 programs. But perhaps point. The point another must answer question from «the automotive world which whole world but only press world—or rather part or better yet single daily newspaper even if Sera end what left? colleague who likes prefabricate domestic drivers travel foreign cars. doesn matter. will answer nine «dramatic» questions from Lorenzo Pilogallo exhaustively because don want disappoint him beating Ford 67 as wrote crazy but finding ourselves up against engineer too would truly end. magazine then includes section dedicated transcript answers (with corresponding questions) given Enzo during conference Terremoto Maranello. Il testo registrato TV Show 66 (tr. Earthquake Maranello. text recorded during Show 66) Autosprint VI no. 52 19-26 1966 pp. 4-6. One journalists Mariani asks Enzo question particular Dino replies as follows Per Dino discorso semplicissimo Fiat sfornerà tra poco Fiat-Dino già stata ammirata al salone Torino conto proprio presentato prototipo suscettibile modifiche dopo le prove stiamo già conducendo strada questo prototipo destinato diventare una macchina produzione massimo 500 unità cioè quel tanto basta ottenerne omologazione nella gran turismo. Quindi dire oggi quando potremo iniziare le consegne queste vetture difficile faccia certo ma previsioni ne possiamo fare comunque ne parlerà nel secondo semestre prossimo anno. English translation As for Dino situation very simple Fiat shortly rolling out Fiat-Dino which has already been admired at Turin Motor Show. independently presented prototype susceptible modifications after tests already conducting road. This prototype destined become production car with maximum 500 units hat just enough obtain homologation grand touring category. Therefore saying today when able start deliveries these cars difficult do certain but cannot make any predictions. In any case discussed second half next year. 19-12-1966 26-12-1966 Rivista settore MAG 0051 Italiano Cartaceo MAG 0051 Historical Archive 42 Photographic inventory 926 Magazines 932 935 934 MAG 0051 no 19 26 1002 mondo! detailsTitle: "È la fine del mondo!", "Terremoto a Maranello",  "Autosprint", year VI, no. 52, December 19-26, 1966
detailsDescription: È la fine del mondo!, open letter by Enzo Ferrari, in "Autosprint", year VI, no. 52, December 19-26, 1966, on the cover.     Text transcription (italian):  Per un momento abbiamo temuto di avere commesso un marchiano errore: infatti l'articolo pubblicato venerdì scorso sul «Corriere della Sera» denunciava clamorosamente che «il mondo dell'automobile» si è posto un interrogativo: Ferrari ha avuto paura?  Occorre spiegare: Ferrari avrebbe avuto quest'anno paura di sottoporsi alle domande della stampa nella conferenza annuale che veniva organizzata in dicembre a Modena. Sarebbe così ricorso all'accorgimento di parlare alla televisione, anzi, per dirla come è stata scritta, si sarebbe attaccato alle gonne di mamma televisione. Il «mondo dell'automobile» è stupito e attonito di questa prassi inusitata, si sarebbe indignato e chiederebbe riparazione.  Noi ci siamo preoccupati, perché in effetti anche noi facciamo un poco parte del mondo dell'automobile e ci sarebbe veramente dispiaciuto se per ventura avessimo mostrata di non saperci stare in questo mondo. Abbiamo precisato il programma 1967 della Ferrari attraverso una trasmissione televisiva. In trentadue minuti, grazie all'organizzazione offerta dal Dott. Assetta della direzione RAI-TV di Torino, ci è stato consentito di esporre le nostre previsioni per la incipiente stagione sportiva.  Dipendesse solo da noi non avremmo fatto neanche questa, perché nella nostra età è quella che veramente ci interessa è, se ne siamo ancora capaci, onorare in pista il nostro Sport. Tutto il resto — lo abbiamo ripetutamente affermato — appartiene ad un ruolo che ci viene troppo spesso perentoriamente attribuito e che noi invece respingiamo.  E' stato sentenziato che la Ferrari è ormai «res publica» e non può sottrarsi a certi doveri ed impegni; ha l'obbligo di informare, di dare notizia su tutto quello che sta facendo, sulle automobili che costruisce, sui piloti ingaggiati, sugli impianti realizzati, sul personale assunto, sul tempo che fa, su quello che farà! Tutto quello che riguarda Maranello fa notizia, e bisogna farne sapere; altrimenti il «mondo dell'automobile» si indigna, protesta, minaccia, attua rappresaglie.  Il bello è che il «mondo dell'automobile» vorrebbe primizie, «qualche cosa che non abbiano tutti». Non ci siamo mai chiesti, ascoltando queste profferire, se si tratti di amore per il giornale, di attivismo professionale, di caccia alla bracconiera, di volontà ambiziosa nel risultare il primo della classe. Non lo sappiamo e preferiamo non saperlo.  Quest'anno proprio per evitare supponibili parzialità, abbiamo accettato l'invito della TV e risposto alle domande dei rappresentanti della stampa da essa invitati.  Se la nostra fiacchezza non ha divertito, poco male: noi non siamo del mestiere. Eventualmente, l'anno prossimo, pregheremo Gino Bramieri e Isabella Biagini di elettrizzare il botta e risposta sui programmi Ferrari 1968. Ma, forse, il punto non è questo.  È un altro: dobbiamo rispondere all'interrogativo del «mondo dell'automobile» che non è poi il mondo, ma soltanto la stampa di quel mondo anzi, parte di quella stampa, meglio un quotidiano, anche se il «Corriere della Sera», e alla fine cosa resta? Un collega che ama prefabbricare piloti di casa e viaggiare su automobili straniere.  Non fa niente. Risponderemo esaurientemente alle nove «drammatiche» domande di Lorenzo Pilogallo, perché non vogliamo deluderlo: batterci nel '67 con la Ford - abbiamo scritto - è pazzesco; ma se ci trovassimo contro anche l'ingegnere Pilogallo sarebbe proprio la fine.     English translation:  For a moment, we feared we had committed a serious error: in fact, the article published last Friday in the «Corriere della Sera» dramatically denounced that «the automotive world» had posed a question: Was Ferrari afraid?  It must be explained: Ferrari was allegedly afraid this year of submitting to the press's questions at the annual conference that was organized every December in Modena. It supposedly resorted to the ploy of speaking on television—or, to quote exactly how it was written, it clung to the apron strings of mommy television. The «automotive world» is astonished and amazed by this unusual practice, it is allegedly indignant and would demand reparations.  We became concerned because, in effect, we too are somewhat part of the automotive world, and we would be truly displeased if we happened to show that we don't know how to behave in this world. We clarified Ferrari's 1967 program through a televised broadcast. In thirty-two minutes, thanks to the organization offered by Dr. Assetta of the RAI-TV management in Turin, we were able to present our forecasts for the upcoming racing season.  If it were solely up to us, we wouldn't have even done this, because at our age, what truly interests us is, if we are still capable, to honor our Sport on the track. All the rest - as we have repeatedly stated - belongs to a role that is too often peremptorily attributed to us and which we, instead, reject.  It has been decreed that Ferrari is now a «res publica» and cannot shirk certain duties and commitments; it has the obligation to inform, to give notice of everything it is doing, about the cars it builds, the drivers it hires, the facilities it creates, the personnel it hires, the weather, and what it will do! Everything concerning Maranello makes news, and people must be informed; otherwise, the «automotive world» becomes indignant, protests, threatens, and enacts reprisals.  The funny thing is that the «automotive world» wants scoops, «something that not everyone has.» We have never asked, listening to these pronouncements, whether it is about love for the newspaper, professional activism, chasing after poachers, or an ambitious desire to come out first in the class. We don't know, and we prefer not to know.  This year, precisely to avoid conceivable partiality, we accepted the invitation of the TV and answered the questions from the representatives of the press invited by them.  If our lack of flair was not entertaining, no matter: we are not professionals. Perhaps next year, we will ask Gino Bramieri and Isabella Biagini to electrify the back-and-forth about the 1968 Ferrari programs. But perhaps, that is not the point.  The point is another: we must answer the question from «the automotive world,» which is not the whole world, but only the press of that world—or rather, a part of that press, or better yet, a single daily newspaper, even if it is the «Corriere della Sera,» and in the end, what is left? A colleague who likes to prefabricate domestic drivers and travel in foreign cars.  It doesn't matter. We will answer all nine «dramatic» questions from Lorenzo Pilogallo exhaustively, because we don't want to disappoint him: beating Ford in '67 - as we wrote - is crazy; but finding ourselves up against engineer Pilogallo, too, would truly be the end.     The magazine then includes a section dedicated to the transcript of the answers (with corresponding questions) given by Enzo Ferrari during the press conference:Terremoto a Maranello. Il testo registrato alla TV del Ferrari Show '66 (tr. Earthquake in Maranello. The text recorded on TV during the Ferrari Show '66), in "Autosprint", year VI, no. 52, December 19-26, 1966, pp. 4-6.  One of the journalists, Mariani, asks Enzo Ferrari a question in particular about the Dino; Ferrari replies as follows:  Per la Dino, il discorso è semplicissimo: la Fiat sfornerà tra poco la Fiat-Dino che è già stata ammirata al salone di Torino, la Ferrari per conto proprio ha presentato un prototipo, suscettibile di modifiche dopo le prove, che noi stiamo già conducendo su strada; questo prototipo è destinato a diventare una macchina di produzione per un massimo di 500 unità, cioè quel tanto che basta per ottenerne la omologazione nella gran turismo. Quindi dire oggi quando noi potremo iniziare le consegne di queste vetture è difficile, che noi si faccia è certo, ma previsioni non ne possiamo fare, comunque se ne parlerà nel secondo semestre del prossimo anno.     English translation:  As for the Dino, the situation is very simple: Fiat will shortly be rolling out the Fiat-Dino, which has already been admired at the Turin Motor Show. Ferrari has independently presented a prototype, which is susceptible to modifications after the tests we are already conducting on the road. This prototype is destined to become a production car with a maximum of 500 units - hat is, just enough to obtain homologation in the grand touring category. Therefore, saying today when we will be able to start deliveries of these cars is difficult; that we will do it is certain, but we cannot make any predictions. In any case, it will be discussed in the second half of next year.

url.original: https://f1947.archivio.com/Archive/Varie/Cars/Dino 206 GT.png

detailsDescription: For Ferrari it was becoming imperative to increase sales and reduce production costs. With the prototype of the Dino 196 S already in-house, there was an excellent opportunity to design a relatively small displacement, road-going version which, for the first time in Ferrari history, could be built on an assembly line. Pininfarina came up with pretty, classic lines and the Fiat V6 was developed by Ferrari and engineered for transverse mounting amidships.    The model met some resistance amongst purists to begin with as it was not considered a ‘real’ Ferrari, but opinions soon changed once clients got the chance to see it close up and drive it. The small-engined Ferrari (relative to other models in the range at the time) came about due to regulations in Formula 2 monoposto racing for 1967, requiring that engines in racing cars had to be production-based, and produced in quantities of no fewer than 500 units a year. At the time, Ferrari could not hope to build that number of suitable units, so to enable them to compete, an alliance was forged with Fiat. Fiat would manufacture the engines and fit them into an upper market range of front-engined cars of their own, using the Dino name, and also supply engines to Ferrari for their own use.    The Dino name was first used on Ferrari cars with vee engines in the late fifties, on Formula 1 and sports-racing models. It was the Christian name of the son of Enzo Ferrari, who had died in 1956, and was used in his memory as he was working on a vee-engine project prior to his death. It continued to be used to designate any model with a vee engine throughout the early sixties, and then again in 1965 on the 166P mid-engined sports-racing model, which evolved into the 206 SP and 206 S sports-racing models. This was the first model to carry a Dino badge on the nose instead of a Ferrari one. The badge was a horizontal rectangle with his stylised script signature in blue on a yellow background.     Later in 1965, at the Paris Salon, a mid-engined prototype was shown on the Pininfarina stand, called the ‘Dino 206 GT Speciale’, which was a design study for a road car, based on the chassis of a sports-racing model, with an in-line, mid-engined placement. It featured headlights under a clear perspex cover that extended the full width of the nose, but otherwise the shape was essentially that which would develop into the production road car.  The cabin featured a shallow, vertical concave curved rear screen between sail panel buttresses that ran into a Kamm tail.  The prototype carried strong influences from the sports racing model, and the larger-engined Ferrari 412P, and 330 P4 sports-racers. This was particularly evident in the shape of the nose, with bulbous wings rolling down into a low front lid line, and the cabin sail panels sweeping down into a Kamm tail. (ferrari.com)
valveActuation: twin overhead camshaft per bank, two valves per cylinder
ignition: single spark plug per cylinder, single coil

detailsTitle: "Ecco la nostra Dino!", in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 1, January 3-9, 1967
detailsDescription: Ecco la nostra Dino! (tr. Here's our Dino!), in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 1, January 3-9, 1967, p.5. Article by the editorial staff of Autosprint magazine reporting on the presentation of the Dino 206/S car at a showcase in the heart of Bologna.  The car is a sports prototype loaned to Autosprint with the aim of involving young drivers who want to enter the world of racing and pursue a career in it.  Specifically, each individual session held at the Modena Aerodrome will be dedicated to a single driver, in order to avoid “the emergence of a competitive atmosphere” from the outset.  In conclusion, the text emphasises the technical characteristics of the vehicle and its performance.

freeSearchText: ID: 1020  Path: 365 GT4 BB (1971) (1020)  365 GT4 BB (1971) At its introduction the 365 GT4 BB also offered additional creature comforts in the form of standard air conditioning radio and electric windows.  The combination of the car’s high performance and Pininfarina’s aggressive styling meant that sales remained relatively buoyant despite recession caused by oil crisis and in three years almost 400 were produced. The 365 GT4BB made its public debut on Pininfarina stand at 1971 Turin Show but it would be almost two years after it reached production line where it would run until 1976.  No doubt long gestation period was due to being completely different concept to any previous Ferrari 12-cylinder series production road car with its mid-mounted flat-12 engine in unit with transmission. Ferrari already had vast experience of mid-engine layout with their single-seater sports-racing models together two 365P “Tre Posti” (three seat) concept road cars produced by Pininfarina 1966 V6 Dino series.  However GTB4 “Daytona” was selling well factory no doubt thought prudent to show new car early assess client reaction what was radically different top range model also give them time test extensively. When GT4BB did go into production “Daytona” continued alongside for few months. Between 1973 1976 total 387 examples were produced. The model title followed standard Ferrari practice number “365″ referring swept volume single cylinder number “4″ relating total number camshafts “BB” suffix stood for “Berlinetta Boxer”. “Boxer” part model name reference opposing banks cylinders’ operating order. However name more an analogy company’s flat-12 Formula One engines as engine GT4BB did not operate true boxer engine sequence where opposing pistons travel opposite directions as crankshaft rotates. (ferrari.com) 1973-1984 two-seater berlinetta flat-12 rear longitudinal 4390.35 cc twin overhead camshafts per bank two valves per cylinder four Weber 40 IF3C carburettors Gas 300 km/h 265 kW (360 hp) at 7500 rpm single-plate electronic single spark plug per cylinder discs 387 1160 kg Models Cars GT4 BB 1971 1020

url.original: https://f1947.archivio.com/Archive/Varie/Cars/365GT4 BB.png

detailsDescription: At its introduction, the 365 GT4 BB also offered additional creature comforts in the form of standard air conditioning, a radio and electric windows.  The combination of the car’s high performance and Pininfarina’s aggressive styling meant that sales remained relatively buoyant despite the recession caused by the oil crisis, and in three years almost 400 were produced. The 365 GT4BB made its public debut on the Pininfarina stand at the 1971 Turin Show, but it would be almost two years after it reached the production line, where it would run until 1976.     No doubt the long gestation period was due to it being a completely different concept to any previous Ferrari 12-cylinder series production road car, with its mid-mounted flat-12 engine in unit with the transmission.    Ferrari already had vast experience of the mid-engine layout with their single-seater and sports-racing models, together with the two 365P “Tre Posti” (three seat) concept road cars produced by Pininfarina in 1966, and the V6 Dino series.  However, the 365 GTB4 “Daytona” was selling well, and the factory no doubt thought it prudent to show the new car early, and assess client reaction to what was a radically different top of the range model, and also give them time to test it extensively.    When the 365 GT4BB did go into production, the “Daytona” continued in production alongside it for a few months. Between 1973 and 1976 a total of 387 examples were produced. The model title followed standard Ferrari practice, with the number “365″ referring to the swept volume of a single cylinder, the number “4″ relating to the total number of camshafts, and the “BB” suffix stood for “Berlinetta Boxer”. The “Boxer” part of the model name was a reference to the opposing banks of cylinders’ operating order. However, the name was more an analogy with the company’s flat-12 Formula One engines, as the engine of the 365 GT4BB did not operate in true boxer engine sequence, where opposing pistons travel in opposite directions as the crankshaft rotates. (ferrari.com)
valveActuation: twin overhead camshafts per bank, two valves per cylinder
ignition: electronic, single spark plug per cylinder

detailsDescription: Designed for long distance competition, the 166 MM took its name from one of the world’s most famous road races, the Mille Miglia, in which the Maranello cars were to triumph again and again. Its coachwork was built by Touring using the “Superlight” method. The 166 MM was both extremely light and extremely dynamic whilst still maintaining significant structural stiffness.  The 166 MM’s engine was also modified for run on “normal” commercially available fuel.    On 15 September 1948, the Ferrari brand presented one of its cars for the first time at a motor show, specifically the Turin Motor Show.  This event represented the fulfilment of a dream: after being a driver, team manager and racing car manufacturer, Enzo Ferrari, now the owner of a fully-fledged car manufacturer, presented a model to be produced in a small series at a renowned international event. The car was the 166 MM, an acronym standing for Mille Miglia, the race won the previous May by Clemente Biondetti and Giuseppe Navone in the 166 Inter coupé with Allemano bodywork.    This 166 MM was destined to go down in motoring history. Not only for its sporting successes, but above all because it introduced a new type of car, namely the barchetta. This name, given to it by the press and inspired by its shape, would from then on distinguish all open-top sports cars. This car was very important to Ferrari, as he had high expectations for it. The 166 MM was obviously a very powerful, high-performance vehicle, but its soft, simple lines also gave it a particular elegance and grace.     The 166 MM is the car that sets the guidelines for future road models from Maranello: it is the progenitor, and Enzo Ferrari is well aware of this. The versatile Sports cars, capable of winning on any circuit, permanent, city or dirt track, are now ready to establish themselves in other contexts, in the most prestigious international events. In fact, Concours d'Elegance events are beginning to take hold and are becoming important for approaching new customers and selling various models of customised cars. Many private drivers purchased the 166 MM in its “racing” version to use in competitions, but just as many wealthy customers chose the road version, often requesting various modifications to the exterior and interior.     For the 166 MM Touring Superleggera, Ferrari collaborated with the Milanese coachbuilder that invented the construction method of the same name, already used in the aeronautical industry for the construction of aircraft. The Superleggera manufacturing technique involves the creation of a small-diameter steel tube frame, to which aluminium body panels are applied. In this way, the chassis and body form a single, homogeneous unit, ensuring good structural rigidity.   Twenty-five examples of the 166 MM with Touring Superleggera bodywork were built. One of these was purchased by Giovanni Agnelli.  (ferrari.com)

detailsDescription: The 1954 Paris Motor Show marked a turning point for Maranello’s road car production. The City of Light – and no other setting could have been more appropriate – saw the unveiling of the 250 Europa GT, then called the 250 GT, designed by Pinin Farina. It was the successor to the 250 Europa, introduced in Paris in 1953.     It caught the eye with its sportier lines, along with different engine and chassis options. It was the template for Ferrari’s entire production over the next 10 years, and was the model that summed up Enzo Ferrari’s car concept, all packaged with the typical elegance of the Turin body shop. The 250 GT marked a change in Ferrari’s assembly process, from hand crafted to production line, with a boom in volumes that doubled in just three years. In fact, a few months before the Paris exhibition, Ferrari had expanded the factory, creating a new light alloy foundry, with the aim of increasing production and improving the company’s profitability to finance its racing activities. Just three years after the historic meeting between Enzo Ferrari and Battista “Pinin” Farina in a restaurant in Tortona in 1951, the agreement between two heavyweights of Italian motoring reached its zenith, with the creation of an icon of automotive history.     Until then, Ferrari usually let its customers choose a body shop to complete and customise a car. Often the same model was aesthetically very diverse, with many different details, even in the choice of materials, like aluminium rather than steel for the body. However, Ferrari understood that to make a quantum leap and place his own imprint on his “creations”, to give them a unique character, he needed a single partner: Pinin Farina shared his vision. Grace and power had to go hand in hand in the development of new products, with mechanics and style merged into a single body. Ferrari forged his “trademark” with the 250 GT, defining his product standards for road cars, which would instantly stand out from those of rival companies. The 250 GT was Maranello’s first real GT: successful on the track, in terms of performance, as well as on public roads too. (ferrari.com)
valveActuation: single overhead camshaft per bank, two valves per cylinder
ignition: single spark plug per cylinder, two coils

detailsDescription: 250 GTO was officially unveiled at a press conference on 24 September 1962, presenting the programmes for the season ahead. In fact, the first experimental car had already been on track at Monza in August 1961 with Giancarlo Baghetti and in September, prior to the Italian Grand Prix tests, with Willy Mairesse and Stirling Moss.    The 250 GTO perfectly encapsulated the Ferrari philosophy – performance and design excellence. It was not simply a great tourer, but a real racing car with a number plate. It was an icon, whose sinuous and at the same time aggressive line is still captivating down to this day, and for many regarded as the most beautiful Ferrari of all time. It won everything there was to win on the track, and today is the highest valued historic car in the world – we are talking about figures of €30 million, which are bound to increase. The car was designed by the brilliant Giotto Bizzarrini based on the 250 GT SWB, to race in the World Marques Championship, which from 1962 changed its name, moving over to GT and was divided into categories. The letter O in GTO stands for Omologata (approved), but the 100 examples that were then requested were never produced. There were 36 plus another three equipped with a 4000-cc engine.    Bizzarrini cared about aerodynamics and the vehicle’s lines were sculpted in the wind tunnel. However, the engineer, a native of Livorno, wasn’t there for the launch of the car in February 1962, as he had been part of the core management that Enzo Ferrari decided to renew at the end of 1961. Responsibility for the vehicle and its approval then passed to Mauro Forghieri. Sergio Scaglietti gave the last touches to the body, shaping the metal exposed on the frame, piece by piece, without drawings. The 250 GTO’s great reputation developed on racetracks around the world, and on road courses such as the famous Tour de France Automobile. The 250 GTO delivered three world championships to Maranello from 1962 to 1964, plus many victories in the most prestigious races. In the early season conference, it was the only one that still had a front engine, the 3-litre V12 300 hp designed by Gioacchino Colombo, while the sports cars and F1s had changed to a mid-engine over the wheel axle. A rear spoiler was added before its debut in the 12 Hours of Sebring. In the race, Olivier Gendebien and Phil Hill drove their 250 GTO to second overall and first in the GT category, behind the Ferrari 250 Testa Rossa, which won with Joakim Bonnier and Lucien Bianchi. We can also see that the 250 GTO is a work of art by the fact that this racing model has the largest number of differently coloured liveries, besides the classic red. (ferrari.com)

freeSearchText: ID: 1025  Path: 275 GTB (1964) (1025)  275 GTB (1964) The car came with standard light alloy rims. Including the and II series around 450 cars were built by Scaglietti. Some of these destined for racing came in aluminium with racing suspension and six Weber carburettors and are known as the 275 GTB/C. The 275 GTB berlinetta replaced the 250 GT Lusso at 1964 Paris Salon marking two landmarks in Ferrari’s road car production history. These were use for first time of combined gearbox differential unit in transaxle assembly provision of independent rear suspension.  The company already had plenty experience both systems their competition cars technology which filtered through to road models another case racing improving breed. GTB was much more aggressive appearance than its predecessor with larger radiator opening featuring an egg crate pattern aluminium grille angled rearward at lower edge like hungry mouth bounded by quarter bumpers above them deeply recessed headlights under Plexiglass covers. body featured powerful curves overall lines that had echoes 250 GTO long forward section set back cabin falling sharply into short Kamm tail carrying circular combination tail/turn light units on lightly recessed panel similar to that its predecessor. body was designed by Pininfarina constructed Scaglietti normally steel aluminium doors bonnet boot lid although some examples received full bodies. cabin was three window design large deeply curved windscreen an almost flat rear screen bounded sail panels that featured triple cabin exhaust air slots matched quadruple arrangement on front wings. year after its introduction model underwent facelift which again shown for first time at Paris Salon.  most noticeable revision provision new longer slimmer nose flat bonnet replacing lightly raised centre section unit on “short nose” car. At same time size rear screen increased boot lid hinges became external to increase capacity within. models are generally referred today as “short nose” or “long nose” two cam models. (ferrari.com) 1964-1968 two-seater berlinetta 60° V12 front longitudinal 3285.72 cc single overhead camshaft per bank two valves per cylinder three or six 40 Weber DCN/3 carburettors Gas 258 km/h 206 kW (280 hp) 7600 rpm single-plate single spark plug per cylinder coils discs 01-01-1964 31-12-1968 1964 Paris Motor Show 780 1100 kg Models Cars 1964 1025

url.original: https://f1947.archivio.com/Archive/Varie/Cars/275 GTB.png

detailsDescription: The car came with standard light alloy rims. Including the I and II series, around 450 cars were built by Scaglietti. Some of these, destined for racing, came in aluminium with racing suspension and six Weber carburettors, and are known as the 275 GTB/C.    The 275 GTB berlinetta replaced the 250 GT Lusso at the 1964 Paris Salon, marking two landmarks in Ferrari’s road car production history. These were the use for the first time of a combined gearbox and differential unit in a transaxle assembly, and the provision of independent rear suspension.  The company already had plenty of experience of both systems in their competition cars, the technology of which filtered through to the road models, another case of racing improving the breed.    The 275 GTB was much more aggressive in appearance than its predecessor, with a larger radiator opening, featuring an egg crate pattern aluminium grille, angled rearward at the lower edge like a hungry mouth, bounded by quarter bumpers, with above them deeply recessed headlights under Plexiglass covers. The body featured powerful curves with overall lines that had echoes of the 250 GTO, with a long forward section and a set back cabin falling sharply into the short Kamm tail, carrying circular combination tail/turn light units on a lightly recessed panel similar to that of its predecessor. The body was designed by Pininfarina, and constructed by Scaglietti, normally in steel with aluminium doors, bonnet, and boot lid, although some examples received full aluminium bodies. The cabin was a three window design with a large deeply curved windscreen and an almost flat rear screen bounded by sail panels that featured triple cabin exhaust air slots that matched the quadruple arrangement on the front wings.    A year after its introduction the model underwent a facelift, which was again shown for the first time at the Paris Salon.  The most noticeable revision was the provision of a new longer slimmer nose, with a flat bonnet replacing the lightly raised centre section unit on the “short nose” car. At the same time the size of the rear screen was increased, and the boot lid hinges became external to increase the capacity within. The models are generally referred to today as “short nose” or “long nose” two cam models. (ferrari.com)
valveActuation: single overhead camshaft per bank, two valves per cylinder
fuelSupply: three or six 40 Weber DCN/3 carburettors
ignition: single spark plug per cylinder, two coils

freeSearchText: ID: 1026  Path: 308 GTB (1975) (1026)  308 GTB (1975) The long awaited replacement for the Dino 246 GT model made its inaugural appearance at the 1975 Paris Salon. The Pininfarina designed body had pronounced wedge profile with rectangular egg-crate aluminium radiator grille below slim full width satin black front bumper. However there were numerous key design elements of the Dino 246 GT carried through into body details. These included scalloped door intakes twin circular rear light assemblies and vertical concave rear screen bounded by buttressed sail panels. In essence shape was modernisation of that of Dino with enough traces its predecessor to provide thread continuity earning praise from press and clients alike. One feature that was not immediately apparent was that 308 GTB fitted with totally fibreglass body apart from aluminium front lid. This first Ferrari production car to feature fibreglass as material and in fact idea has not been repeated by company in large volume production. However individual fibreglass panels have been used on many cars from then until now particularly for front rear valances nose sections. Although standard finish very high return to more traditional pressed steel aluminium happened in late 1976 for USA cars around mid 1977 European models. The simplest way identify bodied car is see if there is an indent line between screen pillar roof panel. If there is one then fibreglass. USA market cars can be identified by heavier bumper assemblies rectangular side marker lights on wings. An optional deep spoiler became available during 1977 which like standard shallow spoiler moulding. Like series luggage compartment provided tail car behind engine bay. On 308 GTB it accessed lifting entire engine cover which revealed zip top compartment whereas models had separate lid luggage area.The main European market models had tubular chassis factory type reference 106 AB 100. Disc brakes independent suspension via wishbones coil springs hydraulic shock absorbers were provided all round anti roll bars.All models were numbered Ferrari odd number road chassis sequence both right left hand drive available. Production ran 1975 through 1980 during which time 2897 examples produced chassis number range 18677 34349. url.original: https://f1947.archivio.com/Archive/Varie/Cars/308 GTB.png

detailsDescription: The long awaited replacement for the Dino 246 GT model made its inaugural appearance at the 1975 Paris Salon. The Pininfarina designed body had a pronounced wedge profile, with a rectangular egg-crate aluminium radiator grille below a slim full width satin black front bumper. However, there were numerous key design elements of the Dino 246 GT carried through into the body details. These included the scalloped door intakes, twin circular rear light assemblies, and the vertical concave rear screen bounded by buttressed sail panels. In essence the shape was a modernisation of that of the Dino, with enough traces of its predecessor to provide a thread of continuity, earning praise from the press and clients alike.    One feature that was not immediately apparent, was that the 308 GTB was fitted with a totally fibreglass body, apart from the aluminium front lid. This was the first Ferrari production car to feature fibreglass as a body material, and in fact the idea has not been repeated by the company in large volume production. However, individual fibreglass panels have been used on many cars from then until now, particularly for the front and rear valances and nose sections.    Although the standard of finish was very high, a return to the more traditional pressed steel and aluminium happened in late 1976 for USA cars, and around mid 1977 for European models. The simplest way to identify a fibreglass bodied car, is to see if there is an indent line between the front screen pillar and roof panel. If there is one, then the body is fibreglass. USA market cars can be identified by heavier bumper assemblies, and rectangular side marker lights on the wings. An optional deep front spoiler became available during 1977, which like the standard shallow spoiler was a fibreglass moulding. Like the Dino series, a luggage compartment was provided in the tail of the car behind the engine bay. On the 308 GTB it was accessed by lifting the entire engine cover, which revealed a zip top compartment, whereas the Dino models had a separate lid for the luggage area.The main European market 308 GTB models had a tubular chassis with factory type reference F 106 AB 100.    Disc brakes, with independent suspension via wishbones, coil springs, and hydraulic shock absorbers, were provided all round, with front and rear anti roll bars.All models were numbered in the Ferrari odd number road car chassis sequence, with both right and left hand drive available. Production ran from 1975 through to 1980, during which time 2897 examples were produced in the chassis number range 18677 to 34349. (ferrari.com)

ignition: single spark plug per cylinder, single coil

The successor to the F512M the 550 Maranello proved quicker and thanks to its better front-engined rear drive packaging layout far more practical. After over two decades of Ferrari production with mid engine model as performance pinnacle regular production range tables were turned in 1996 with introduction Maranello. The new model had front mounted engine like its 456 GT stable mate and had been developed from technological progress and innovations that model produce searing performance allied impeccable road manners. made its public debut at major Ferrari gathering at Nurburgring Germany in July 1996 which centred round launch new car. then current F1 team drivers Michael Schumacher Eddie Irvine were on hand put car through paces on track along with host previous Ferrari team drivers including former World Champions Niki Lauda Jody Scheckter. was produced between 1996 2001 all were numbered in continuous chassis number road car sequence range 99928 126807. In 1999 limited series thirty three “World Record” examples produced commemorate World Speed Record set at Marysville USA on 12 October 1998. Here broke world speed record for production road cars covering 100kms an average speed 304.1kph (192.60mph) covering 296.168kms (184.164 miles) one hour despite making stop refuel. 27 November issue British magazine “Autocar” featured test where they recorded 0-60 mph time 4.6 seconds 0-100 mph time 10.1 seconds quoting manufacturer’s claimed top speed 199 mph. They also proclaimed it “Britain’s Best Driver’s Car 1998″ 1999 said it “it continues be best GT block”. url.original: https://f1947.archivio.com/Archive/Varie/Cars/550 Maranello.png

detailsDescription: The 550 Maranello was Ferrari’s answer to those who believed the performance of a front-engined V12 car could not beat that of a mid-engined sports car. The successor to the F512M, the 550 Maranello proved quicker and, thanks to its better front-engined, rear drive packaging layout, far more practical.    After over two decades of Ferrari production with a mid engine model as the performance pinnacle of the regular production range, the tables were turned in 1996 with the introduction of the 550 Maranello. The new model had a front mounted engine like its 456 GT stable mate, and had been developed from the technological progress and innovations of that model, to produce searing performance allied to impeccable road manners.    The 550 Maranello made its public debut at a major Ferrari gathering at the Nurburgring, Germany, in July 1996, which centred round the launch of the new car. The then current F1 team drivers Michael Schumacher and Eddie Irvine were on hand to put the car through its paces on the track, along with a host of previous Ferrari team drivers including former Ferrari World Champions Niki Lauda and Jody Scheckter.    The 550 Maranello was produced between 1996 and 2001, and all were numbered in the continuous chassis number road car sequence in the range 99928 to 126807. In 1999 a limited series of thirty three “World Record” examples were produced to commemorate the World Speed Record set at Marysville in the USA on 12 October 1998. Here the 550 Maranello broke the world speed record for a production road cars, covering 100kms at an average speed of 304.1kph (192.60mph), and covering 296.168kms (184.164 miles) in one hour, despite making a stop to refuel. The 27 November 1996 issue of the British magazine “Autocar” featured a road test, where they recorded a 0-60 mph time of 4.6 seconds, a 0-100 mph time of 10.1 seconds, quoting the manufacturer’s claimed top speed of 199 mph. They also proclaimed it “Britain’s Best Driver’s Car 1998″, and in 1999 said of it “it continues to be the best GT on the block”. (ferrari.com)

freeSearchText: ID: 1028  Path: Monza SP1 (2018) (1028)  Monza SP1 (2018) The Ferrari Monza SP1 together with the Monza SP2 are the forerunners in new concept known as ‘Icona’ (Icon) that taps into leitmotif of the most evocative cars in company’s history to create new segment of special limited series cars for clients and collectors. The intention is to use modern aesthetic to reinterpret timeless style with technologically advanced components and highest performance possible through continuous innovation. The intention is to use a modern aesthetic to reinterpret a timeless style, with technologically advanced components and the highest performance possible through continuous innovation.

February 20-27 1967 Ore 13 lezione col DINO (tr. p.m. Lesson with DINO) in Autosprint year VII no. February 20-27 1967 p. 7. Autosprint magazine reports the launching of the driver training program at the Modena circuit for young talents identified in Casco Oro competition. Utilizing Dino 2000 spider and Ferrari technical support (led by Florini) initiative aims to help drivers gain confidence with prototype safe conditions. The goal is progressive adaptation and continuous work rather than immediate record times differentiating it from short hill climb races. Vittorio Venturi completed over 200 km demonstrating remarkable emotional composure and good adaptation to powerful car. 1967 20-02-1967 27-02-1967 Rivista M.S. detailsTitle: "Ore 13 lezione col DINO", in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 8, February 20-27, 1967
detailsDescription: Ore 13 lezione col DINO (tr. 1 p.m. Lesson with DINO) in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 8, February 20-27, 1967, p. 7. Autosprint magazine reports the launching of the driver training program at the Modena circuit for young talents, identified in the "Casco d'Oro" competition. Utilizing a Dino 2000 spider and Ferrari's technical support (led by Florini), the initiative aims to help drivers gain confidence with the prototype in safe conditions. The goal is progressive adaptation and continuous work, rather than immediate record times, differentiating it from short hill climb races. Vittorio Venturi completed over 200 km, demonstrating remarkable emotional composure and good adaptation to the powerful car.

Reflecting on Italian drivers he laments the decline of dedicated professional sportsmen in modern racing criticizing the lack of rigorous training compared to past eras suggesting that contemporary racing primarily serves car development rather than driver formation. He recalls legendary drivers like Felice Nazzaro Lorenzo Bandini whose styles fostered professional wheelmen pursuit hindered the lack of Italian racetracks. proposes Machine Championship involve construction permanent technically designed roads with emergency services attributing current road racing limitations Minister Scalfaro. detailsTitle: "Ferrari otto e mezzo", in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 33-34, August 21-28, 1967
detailsDescription: Ferrari otto e mezzo (tr. Ferrari eight and a half) in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 33-34, August 21-28, 1967, p. 3.  The Autosprint interview with Enzo Ferrari by Marcellino delves into his business philosophy and personal perspective on racing. Ferrari dismisses retirement rumors, affirming his continued active role despite evolving challenges. He views competition, exemplified by collaborations like with Firestone, as vital for automotive progress and product recognition, prioritizing field experience over laboratory research. While acknowledging that Ferrari could theoretically shift to solely producing "gran turismo" cars for profit, he emphatically identifies as a "builder" rather than an "industrialist," valuing creation over mere quantity and defining his identity by this distinction. Ferrari also expresses indifference to public perception, focusing instead on applying his capabilities.    Reflecting on Italian drivers, he laments the decline of dedicated "professional sportsmen" in modern racing, criticizing the lack of rigorous training compared to past eras and suggesting that contemporary racing primarily serves car development rather than driver formation. He recalls legendary drivers like Felice Nazzaro and Lorenzo Bandini, whose styles fostered professional wheelmen, a pursuit hindered by the lack of Italian racetracks. Ferrari proposes a "Machine Championship" to involve construction and permanent, technically designed roads with emergency services, attributing current road racing limitations to Minister Scalfaro. The interview concludes provocatively, questioning Ferrari's omission of Nuvolari from his recollections and his stance on speed limits and the utility of high-powered cars on normal roads.

detailsTitle: "Il salone secondo le tasche", in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 44, October 20 - November 6, 1967
detailsDescription: Il salone secondo le tasche (tr. The Salone according to your budget) in Autosprint, year VII, no. 44, October 20 - November 6, 1967, pp. 23-24.  Presentation of the Dino coupé model at the 1967 Turin Motor Show: this is the Dino GT, which differs from the previous model in terms of a number of stylistic features designed by Pininfarina, but above all in terms of the engine transmission. In fact, it has been placed at the rear in a crosswise position: a solution that allows for more efficient use of space.  The technical data also shows the cost of the car: L. 4,950,000.  The article also details the Ferrari 275 GTB 4, the latest and most powerful version of the GTB line, distinguished by the twin-cam engines mounted under the bonnet, usually reserved for prototypes. The technical data also shows the cost of the car: L. 6,500,000.

detailsTitle: "Auto contro auto", in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 46, November 13-20, 1967
detailsDescription: Auto contro auto (tr. Car versus car) in "Autosprint", year VII, no. 46, November 13-20, 1967, pp. 8-11. In-depth article dedicated to the 49th Turin Motor Show in 1967, during which Ferrari presented the Dino 206 GT, Porsche's rival. In the paragraph entitled “The 1968 cars as seen by the car body designers...”, the text reports on the strong presence of Italian car body designers. At the Pininfarina stand, the Dino 206 GT triumphed: a coupé that combined engine power with an aerodynamic design featuring a low, streamlined body.

freeSearchText: ID: 1102  Path: MAG 0118 "Ferrari 70", in "Autosprint", year VIII, no. 8, February 19-26, 1968 (1102)  Ferrari 70 in Autosprint year VIII no. February 19-26 1968 Ferrari 70 in Autosprint year VIII no. February 19-26 1968 pp. 13-19.  The article celebrates Enzo Ferrari 70th birthday through the personal memories of its author Marcello Sabbatini. The journalist recalls the atmosphere of pre-war racing citing legendary drivers like Nuvolari and historic circuits like the Coppa Acerbo. In that context was already an authoritative and formidable figure observed with admiration. The piece focuses on famous definition himself gave of his life journey marked by terrible joys. It analyzes stark contrast between great successes achieved worldwide and personal professional tragedies that have marked his existence. What emerges is portrait man with demanding complex character whose story is not that flawless hero but strong personality who dedicated his life to mission with absolute rigor. article underscores how career was an all-encompassing choice led him to turn down secure position at Fiat to become world-renowned constructor whose name is synonymous cars. Black white photographs from Walter Breviglieri archive reproduce moments from Enzo life from childhood 1960s.  February 19-26 1968 19-02-1968 26-02-1968 Rivista Gianni Cancellieri Marcello Sabbatini Autosprint MAG 0118 Italiano Cartaceo Historical Archive 42 Photographic inventory 926 Magazines 932 935 1090 MAG 0118 year VIII no 19 26 1101 MAG 0118 70 in no 19 26 1102

detailsDescription: Ferrari 70, in "Autosprint", year VIII, no. 8, February 19-26, 1968, pp. 13-19.     The article celebrates Enzo Ferrari's 70th birthday through the personal memories of its author, Marcello Sabbatini. The journalist recalls the atmosphere of pre-war racing, citing legendary drivers like Nuvolari and historic circuits like the Coppa Acerbo. In that context, Ferrari was already an authoritative and formidable figure, observed with admiration.  The piece focuses on the famous definition Ferrari himself gave of his life: a journey marked by "terrible joys." It analyzes the stark contrast between the great successes achieved worldwide and the personal and professional tragedies that have marked his existence. What emerges is the portrait of a man with a demanding and complex character, whose story is not that of a flawless hero, but of a strong personality who dedicated his life to his mission with absolute rigor.  The article underscores how his career was an all-encompassing choice, that led him to turn down a secure position at Fiat to become a world-renowned constructor, whose name is synonymous with his cars.    Black and white photographs from Walter Breviglieri's archive reproduce moments from Enzo Ferrari's life, from his childhood to the 1960s.

The section titled The Mosquito (with is presented as an anecdote captured behind the scenes at the Mugello circuit. It describes scene of clear frustration featuring driver Radicella team. Below are some brief comments on situations or matches such as the one commented on in purple ink  ‘Sabbatini for everyone next up!’ refers to short article ‘At headquarters of Bardhal racing team Florence you can see Pirelli tyre that exploded at 140 km/h.’ February 19-26 1968 12-08-1968 26-08-1968 Rivista Marcello Sabbatini Autosprint MAG 0143 Italiano Cartaceo Ferrari Historical Archive 42 Photographic inventory 926 Magazines 932 935 1090 MAG 0143 year VIII no 33 34 August 12 26 1127 MAG 0143 La zanzara con microcamera no 19 26 1128

detailsTitle: "La zanzara (con microcamera)", in "Autosprint", year VIII, no. 8, February 19-26, 1968
detailsDescription: La zanzara (con microcamera) (tr. The Mosquito (with a microcamera), in "Autosprint", year VIII, no. 8, February 19-26, 1968, p. 3.  The section, titled "The Mosquito (with a microcamera)," is presented as an anecdote captured behind the scenes at the Mugello circuit. It describes a scene of clear frustration featuring driver Radicella's team. Below are some brief comments on situations or matches, such as the one commented on in purple ink: ‘Sabbatini for everyone: next up!’ refers to the short article, ‘At the headquarters of the Bardhal racing team in Florence, you can see a Pirelli tyre that exploded at 140 km/h.’

(1143)  Si guarda Dino si marcia Ferrari in Autosprint Settimanale di automobilismo sportivo year VIII no. 48 November 25 December 1968. Si guarda Dino si marcia Ferrari (tr. Look like Dino march Ferrari) in Autosprint Settimanale di automobilismo sportivo year VIII no. 48 November 25 December 1968 pp. 26-27.  The article written in the first person by professional driver Andrea de Adamich is an account of an exceptional test he used a Dino 206 GT as his personal car for an entire month covering approximately 15 000 kilometers all conditions from the city to the highway. The title You drive you march Ferrari perfectly summarizes his conclusion although badge on hood is soul character and driving experience are unequivocally those of true Ferrari. De Adamich describes not as car to be used but as car to be piloted. The driving position is that of race almost lying down with offset pedals typical competition vehicles. This makes it unique experience very different from conventional grand tourer. sound V6 engine one most exhilarating elements. author defines it as roar more reminiscent single-seater than road pleasure for driver and magnet for gazes passersby. 1968 25-11-1968 02-12-1968 Rivista Andrea de Adamich Autosprint MAG 0157 Italiano Cartaceo Historical Archive 42 Photographic inventory 926 Magazines 932 935 1968 1090 MAG 0157 year VIII no 48 November 25 December 1142 MAG 0157 Si guarda si marcia Settimanale di automobilismo sportivo no 1143

url.original: https://f1947.archivio.com/SYSTEM - Archivio Storico Pubblico Ferrari (Ente Comunicazione)/PHYSICAL COMMUNICATION ARCHIVE/MAGAZINE/MAG_0157/MAG_0157_002.jpeg
https://f1947.archivio.com/SYSTEM - Archivio Storico Pubblico Ferrari (Ente Comunicazione)/PHYSICAL COMMUNICATION ARCHIVE/MAGAZINE/MAG_0157/MAG_0157_003.jpeg

detailsTitle: "Si guarda Dino, si marcia Ferrari", in Autosprint - Settimanale di automobilismo sportivo, year VIII, no. 48, November 25 - December 2, 1968.
detailsDescription: Si guarda Dino, si marcia Ferrari (tr. Look like Dino, march Ferrari), in Autosprint - Settimanale di automobilismo sportivo, year VIII, no. 48, November 25 - December 2, 1968, pp. 26-27.     The article, written in the first person by professional driver Andrea de Adamich, is an account of an exceptional test: he used a Dino 206 GT as his personal car for an entire month, covering approximately 15,000 kilometers in all conditions, from the city to the highway. The title, "You drive a Dino, you march Ferrari," perfectly summarizes his conclusion: although the badge on the hood is Dino, the soul, character, and driving experience are unequivocally those of a true Ferrari.    De Adamich describes the Dino not as a car to be used, but as a car to be "piloted." The driving position is that of a race car, almost lying down, with the offset pedals typical of competition vehicles. This makes it a unique experience, very different from a conventional grand tourer.  The sound of the V6 engine is one of the most exhilarating elements. The author defines it as a "roar" more reminiscent of a single-seater than a road car, a pleasure for the driver and a magnet for the gazes of passersby.

preferredLabel: "La sua gioia terribile", "Quarant'anni fa nasceva la Scuderia Ferrari", in "Autosprint", year IX, no. 25, June 23-30, 1969
freeSearchText: ID: 1177  Path: MAG 0186  "La sua gioia terribile", "Quarant'anni fa nasceva la Scuderia Ferrari", in "Autosprint", year IX, no. 25, June 23-30, 1969 (1177)  La sua gioia terribile Quarant anni fa nasceva la Scuderia Ferrari in Autosprint year IX no. 25 June 23-30 1969 1)  Marcello Sabbatini  La sua gioia terribile. La FIAT entro dicembre assorbirà del tutto la FERRARI (tr. His terrible joy. FIAT will completely absorb FERRARI by December) in Autosprint year IX no. 25 June 23-30 1969 pp. 2-3 detailed article about the FIAT-FERRARI agreement.  The article titled His Terrible Joy describes the backstory of secret plan orchestrated by Fiat President Gianni Agnelli to secure almost total control of Ferrari for Fiat. The operation takes place after long period of intense media speculation to which Agnelli and Fiat management responded with strict silence. The piece analyzes the complex financial maneuver through which Fiat is about to finalize an agreement that will take it beyond its previous 50% stake moving towards definitive shareholding that will guarantee full control Maranello-based company especially after future passing Enzo Ferrari. 2) Mario Morselli  Quarant anni fa nasceva la Scuderia Ferrari (tr. Forty years ago Scuderia Ferrari was founded)  in Autosprint year IX no. 25 June 23-30 1969 p. 3. article celebrates fortieth anniversary birth founded in Modena 1930. author Mario Morselli looks back with admiration at glorious stages scuderia emphasizing how Enzo always demonstrated vision oriented towards future and open young people. 23-06-1969 30-06-1969 Rivista Marcello Sabbatini Mario Morseli MAG 0186 Italiano Cartaceo MAG 0186 Historical Archive 42 Photographic inventory 926 Magazines 932 935 1149 MAG 0186 no 23 30 1176 La sua gioia terribile Quarant anni fa nasceva no 23 30 1177

url.original: https://f1947.archivio.com/SYSTEM - Archivio Storico Pubblico Ferrari (Ente Comunicazione)/PHYSICAL COMMUNICATION ARCHIVE/MAGAZINE/MAG_0186/MAG_0186_002.jpeg

detailsTitle: "La sua gioia terribile", "Quarant'anni fa nasceva la Scuderia Ferrari", in "Autosprint", year IX, no. 25, June 23-30, 1969
detailsDescription: 1)  Marcello Sabbatini, La sua gioia terribile. La FIAT entro dicembre assorbirà del tutto la FERRARI (tr. His terrible joy. FIAT will completely absorb FERRARI by December), in "Autosprint", year IX, no. 25, June 23-30, 1969, pp. 2-3: detailed article about the FIAT-FERRARI agreement.     The article, titled "His Terrible Joy," describes the backstory of a "secret plan" orchestrated by Fiat's President, Gianni Agnelli, to secure almost total control of Ferrari for Fiat. The operation takes place after a long period of intense media speculation, to which Agnelli and Fiat's management responded with strict silence.  The piece analyzes the complex financial maneuver through which Fiat is about to finalize an agreement that will take it beyond its previous 50% stake, moving towards a definitive shareholding that will guarantee full control of the Maranello-based company, especially after the future passing of Enzo Ferrari.   2) Mario Morselli, Quarant'anni fa nasceva la Scuderia Ferrari (tr. Forty years ago, Scuderia Ferrari was founded), in "Autosprint", year IX, no. 25, June 23-30, 1969, p. 3.    The article celebrates the fortieth anniversary of the birth of Scuderia Ferrari, founded in Modena in 1930. The author, Mario Morselli, looks back with admiration at the glorious stages of the scuderia, emphasizing how Enzo Ferrari always demonstrated a vision oriented towards the future and open to young people.

detailsTitle: "Le 20 spine di Maranello", in "Autosprint", year X, no. 41, October 11-18, 1971
detailsDescription: Le 20 spine di Maranello (tr. The twenty thorns of Maranello) in "Autosprint", year X, no. 41, October 11-18, 1971, pp. 14-17.  This extensive Autosprint interview with Enzo Ferrari paints a vivid picture of his complex identity as both a pragmatic industrialist and an unwavering racing enthusiast. Ferrari firmly asserts that while his road car factory provides the financial backbone, it's his "passion" for discovery and technical development that drives his racing endeavors, famously stating that if he were merely an accountant, he "wouldn't race." This highlights his unique approach, where racing is not just a marketing tool but a personal crusade.    A central theme is the crucial relationship between the driver and the machine. Ferrari conceptualizes drivers as "heat conductors" – vital conduits transmitting critical information from the car to the technical team. He expresses a strong preference for "training his own drivers" over acquiring established champions, seeking deep loyalty and a symbiotic bond with the vehicle, and openly criticizing a purely transactional, mercenary attitude in drivers.    Ferrari views racing as an indispensable "laboratory" for continuous technical innovation, emphasizing that the true work of progress begins "the Monday after the race." He critiques the increasing trend of racing becoming overly commercialized and a mere "spectacle," advocating for a return to its fundamental purpose as a genuine "test of man and machine." He acknowledges the financial reliance on road car sales and accepts "deserved" criticism but remains resolute in his vision.    His leadership style underscores direct involvement ("the eye and hand of the 'master'"), crucial for guiding the team and ensuring continuous progress. Ferrari also touches upon the need for adapting historic circuits like Monza to modern safety standards and implicitly defends his choices in driver selection and team management by prioritizing dedication and integration over sheer talent. The interview ultimately reinforces Ferrari's enduring commitment to racing as a relentless pursuit of technical excellence and a profound personal passion.    Enzo Ferrari's quotes:     	 	"Ferrari is an industry; racing serves to give color to what is mass-produced." 	 	 	"If I were an accountant and not in love with racing, I wouldn't race." 	 	 	"The driver must understand the car, and the car must be understood by the driver." 	 	 	"I prefer to train my drivers rather than buy them." 	 	 	"Races have become too much of a spectacle; they must return to being a test of man and machine." 	 	 	"The real work begins on Monday, after the race." 	 	 	"Progress is a flow; it never stops."

preferredLabel: "La rivoluzionaria GT in casa Ferrari", in "Autosprint", Year XI,  no. 44, November 1-8, 1971
freeSearchText: ID: 1300  Path: MAG 0304 "La rivoluzionaria GT in casa Ferrari", in "Autosprint", Year XI,  no. 44, November 1-8, 1971 (1300)  La rivoluzionaria GT in casa Ferrari in Autosprint Year XI no. 44 November 1-8 1971 La rivoluzionaria GT in casa Ferrari (tr. The revolutionary GT from Ferrari) Autosprint Year XI  no. 44 November 1-8 1971 p. 17.  Article reports about the unveiling of the new 12-cylinder Ferrari designed by Pininfarina and named BB during the 53rd Turin Motor Show.  In addition to rear-mid engine new features include high performance on road excellent visibility and comfort including thermal and acoustic comfort.  The bodywork is emphasised by striking aerodynamics refined styling that perfectly reflects car interior exterior.  Another unique feature is rear doors which are hinged against wind can only be opened if front doors have been opened first. The challenge is also to get them approved. 1971 01-11-1971 08-11-1971 Rivista di settore Autosprint MAG 0304 Italiano Cartaceo MAG 0304 Historical Archive 42 Photographic inventory 926 Magazines 932 935 1253 MAG 0304 year XI no 44 November 1299 La rivoluzionaria casa Year no 1300

detailsTitle: "La rivoluzionaria GT in casa Ferrari", in "Autosprint", Year XI,  no. 44, November 1-8, 1971
detailsDescription: La rivoluzionaria GT in casa Ferrari (tr. The revolutionary GT from Ferrari) in "Autosprint", Year XI,  no. 44, November 1-8, 1971, p. 17.  Article reports about the unveiling of the new 12-cylinder Ferrari designed by Pininfarina and named BB during the 53rd Turin Motor Show.  In addition to the rear-mid engine, new features include high performance on the road, excellent visibility and comfort, including thermal and acoustic comfort.     The bodywork is emphasised by striking aerodynamics and refined styling that perfectly reflects the car's interior and exterior.  Another unique feature is the rear doors, which are hinged against the wind and can only be opened if the front doors have been opened first. The challenge is also to get them approved.

freeSearchText: ID: 1325  Path: MAG 0373 "Le somiglianze «parallele»", in "Autosprint mese", year XIII, no. 11, July 1-31, 1973 (1325)  Le somiglianze «parallele» in Autosprint mese year XIII no. 11 July 1-31 1973 M. M. Le somiglianze «parallele» (tr. Parallel similarities) in Autosprint mese year XIII no. 11 July 1-31 1973 pp. 27-31. Le auto (come le canzoni) spesso non sono originalissime mostrano un comune denominatore estetico tecnico. Conseguenza di una moda concorrenza estrema? questo servizio cerca una risposta (english translation Cars (like songs) are often not very original they show common aesthetic or technical denominator. Is this consequence of fashion or extreme competition? This article seeks an answer ). Among the cars compared in which signs of continuity are noted are some models designed by Pininfarina the three-seater Ferrari 365 from 66 the Ferrari Dino 206 GT from 67 and Ferrari 250 P5 which was later transformed into Alfa Romeo 33. July 1-31 1973 01-07-1973 31-07-1973 Rivista di settore M. detailsTitle: "Le somiglianze «parallele»", in "Autosprint mese", year XIII, no. 11, July 1-31, 1973
detailsDescription: M. M., Le somiglianze «parallele» (tr. "Parallel" similarities), in "Autosprint mese", year XIII, no. 11, July 1-31, 1973, pp. 27-31.     "Le auto (come le canzoni) spesso non sono originalissime: mostrano un comune denominatore estetico o tecnico. Conseguenza di una moda o concorrenza estrema? questo servizio cerca una risposta" (english translation: "Cars (like songs) are often not very original: they show a common aesthetic or technical denominator. Is this a consequence of fashion or extreme competition? This article seeks an answer").     Among the cars compared, in which signs of "continuity" are noted, are some models designed by Pininfarina: the three-seater Ferrari 365 P from '66, the Ferrari Dino 206 GT from '67, and the Ferrari 250 P5, which was later transformed into the Alfa Romeo 33.

preferredLabel: "Anche una LOLA 3000 (francese che fuma) alla 24 ORE DAYTONA", in "Autosprint", year XIII, no. 3, January 15-22, 1973
freeSearchText: ID: 1340  Path: MAG 0379  "Anche una LOLA 3000 (francese che fuma) alla 24 ORE DAYTONA", in "Autosprint", year XIII, no. 3, January 15-22, 1973 (1340)  Anche una LOLA 3000 (francese che fuma) alla 24 ORE DAYTONA in Autosprint year XIII no. January 15-22 1973  Anche una LOLA 3000 (francese che fuma) alla 24 ORE DAYTONA (tr. Also LOLA 3000 (...) at the DAYTONA 24 HOURS) in Autosprint year XIII no. January 15-22 1973 p. 17. The North American Racing Team led by Luigi Chinetti will compete at Daytona with two Ferrari Daytona 365 GTB cars driven by as-yet-unnamed pilots. These will be the only Ferrari in the race and prestigious finish could directly earn points for Ferrari despite Chinetti indirect participation. January 15-22 1973 15-01-1973 22-01-1973 Rivista M.M. Autosprint MAG 0379 Italiano Cartaceo MAG 0379 Historical Archive 42 Photographic inventory 926 Magazines 932 935 1311 MAG 0379 year XIII no 15 22 1338 Anche una francese che fuma alla ORE DAYTONA no 15 22 1340
detailsTitle: "Anche una LOLA 3000 (francese che fuma) alla 24 ORE DAYTONA", in "Autosprint", year XIII, no. 3, January 15-22, 1973
detailsDescription: Anche una LOLA 3000 (francese che fuma) alla 24 ORE DAYTONA (tr. Also a LOLA 3000 (...) at the DAYTONA 24 HOURS), in "Autosprint", year XIII, no. 3, January 15-22, 1973, p. 17.     The North American Racing Team, led by Luigi Chinetti, will compete at Daytona with two Ferrari Daytona 365 GTB 4 cars, driven by as-yet-unnamed pilots. These will be the only Ferrari in the race, and a prestigious finish could directly earn points for Ferrari, despite Chinetti's indirect participation.

detailsTitle: "Rubrica Zanzara", in "Autosprint", year XIII, no. 4, January 22-29, 1973
detailsDescription: Zanzara, in "Autosprint", year XIII, no. 4, January 22-29, 1973, p. 3.  Inside the column titled 'Zanzara', there is a photograph of the entrance of the Ferrari Factory with the following caption: "On the eve of the F1 world 'premiere' in Argentina, this 'notice of... search' (WANTED) appeared on one of the trees in front of the 'Cavallino' restaurant in Maranello. (For the record: the well-executed portrait is one of those officially distributed by Clay [Regazzoni]'s new sponsor, which made the 'escape' possible)."

(tr. Agnelli-jet lands in Fiorano (discussion about Ferrari 1973 season?) in Autosprint year XII no. 22 May 29 June 1972 p. 29.  Short article dedicated to Gianni Agnelli sudden visit to the newly built track welcomed by Enzo Ferrari he visited the factory and stayed for just over an hour talking about sports programmes with the technical engineers. url.original: https://f1947.archivio.com/SYSTEM - Archivio Storico Pubblico Ferrari (Ente Comunicazione)/PHYSICAL COMMUNICATION ARCHIVE/MAGAZINE/MAG_0341/MAG_0341_002.jpeg

detailsTitle: "Agnelli-jet piomba a Fiorano (discusso il 1973 della Ferrari?)", in "Autosprint", year XII, no. 22, May 29 - June 3, 1972
detailsDescription: Agnelli-jet piomba a Fiorano (discusso il 1973 della Ferrari?) (tr. Agnelli-jet lands in Fiorano (discussion about Ferrari's 1973 season?) in "Autosprint", year XII, no. 22, May 29 - June 3, 1972, p. 29.  Short article dedicated to Gianni Agnelli's sudden visit to the newly built Fiorano track: welcomed by Enzo Ferrari, he visited the factory and stayed for just over an hour talking about sports programmes with the technical engineers. The journalist speculates that his visit may have had something to do with the recent board meeting ahead of the 1973 programmes.
